name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
لِإِيلَـٰفِ قُرَيْشٍ (١)
```

```english
[In gratitude] for solidarity among the Quraysh, (1)
```

```arabic
إِۦلَـٰفِهِمْ رِحْلَةَ ٱلشِّتَآءِ وَٱلصَّيْفِ (٢)
```

```english
their solidarity during winter and summer journeys, (2)
```

```arabic
فَلْيَعْبُدُوا۟ رَبَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْبَيْتِ (٣)
```

```english
let them worship the Lord of this House, (3)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِىٓ أَطْعَمَهُم مِّن جُوعٍ وَءَامَنَهُم مِّنْ خَوْفٍۭ (٤)
```

```english
who has fed them [and saved them] from hunger, and secured them from fear. (4)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah contains an expression of favor toward the Quraysh regarding their being accustomed (إيلاف) to the two journeys, followed by a call for them to embrace monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and to worship the Lord of the House. The Surah is Meccan.

The content of this Surah bears a certain connection to the content of Surah al-Fil; for this reason, a group among the Sunnis held the view that *al-Fil* and *Li-ilaf* constitute a single Surah. A similar claim was made regarding *al-Duha* and *Alam Nashrah* due to the connection between them, and this view has also been attributed to the popular opinion among the Shi‘a. However, the truth is that none of the evidence they rely upon substantiates this.

As for those among the Sunnis who hold this view, they rely on what has been narrated: that Ubayy ibn Ka‘b did not separate them in his codex (مصحف) with the *Basmalah* (البسملة). They also rely on what was narrated from ‘Amr ibn Maymun al-Azdi, who said: "I prayed the sunset prayer behind ‘Umar ibn al-Khattab, and he recited *al-Tin* in the first unit (ركعة) and *Alam tara* and *Li-ilaf Quraysh* in the second without separating them with the *Basmalah*."

The first narration is answered by the fact that it is contradicted by what was narrated that he did indeed include the *Basmalah* between them in his codex. The second is answered by the possibility—assuming its authenticity—that the narrator did not hear its recitation or that he recited it silently. Furthermore, it is contradicted by what was narrated from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that Allah favored the Quraysh with seven characteristics, among which was: "**And a Surah of the Quran was revealed concerning them in which no one else was mentioned: 'For the accustomedness of the Quraysh...'**" (the hadith). Moreover, the separation between them is established by mass-transmission (تواتر).

As for those among the Shi‘a who hold this view, they rely on what is in *al-Majma‘*, narrated from Abu al-‘Abbas from one of the two [Imams] عليهما‌السلام, who said: "**'Have you not seen how your Lord dealt...' and 'For the accustomedness of the Quraysh' are one Surah.**" They also rely on what is in *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain of transmission to al-‘Ala’ from Zayd al-Shahham, who said: "Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام led us in the dawn prayer and recited *al-Duha* and *Alam Nashrah* in one unit." Also, what is in *al-Majma‘* from al-‘Ayyashi from al-Mufaddal ibn Salih from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "I heard him saying: '**Do not combine two Surahs in a single unit except for *al-Duha* and *Alam Nashrah*, and *Alam tara kayfa* and *Li-ilaf Quraysh*.**'" Al-Muhaqqiq narrated the same in *al-Mu‘tabar*, quoting from the book *al-Jami‘* by Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Abi Nasr from al-Mufaddal.

As for the narration of Abu al-‘Abbas, it is weak (ضعيف) because it is disconnected (الرفع).

As for the narration of al-Shahham, it has been narrated from him through two other paths. One is in *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain to Ibn Muskan from Zayd al-Shahham, who said: "Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام led us in prayer and recited *al-Duha* and *Alam Nashrah* for us." The second is from him, from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr, from some of our companions, from Zayd al-Shahham, who said: "Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام led us in prayer; he recited *al-Duha* in the first [unit] and *Alam nashrah laka sadrak* in the second."

This latter narration—meaning the authentic (صحيحة) report of Ibn Abi ‘Umayr—is explicit regarding the recitation of the two Surahs in two separate units. With this, the narration of al-‘Ala’ no longer clearly indicates combining them. As for the narration of Ibn Muskan, it does not clearly indicate combining them, nor is it explicit. Any attempt to interpret Ibn Abi ‘Umayr's report as referring to a supererogatory prayer (النافلة) is refuted by his saying: "He led us in prayer," which is explicit regarding congregational prayer (الجماعة), and there is no congregation in supererogatory prayers.

As for the narration of al-Mufaddal, it is more indicative of them being two Surahs than one, as it says: "Do not combine two Surahs," and then it exempts *al-Duha* and *Alam Nashrah* from the [prohibition of combining] two Surahs, and likewise *al-Fil* and *Li-ilaf*.

Thus, the truth is that if the narrations indicate anything, they only indicate the permissibility of joining (القرآن) between the two Surahs of *al-Duha* and *Alam Nashrah*, and the two Surahs of *al-Fil* and *Li-ilaf*, in a single unit of the obligatory prayers (الفرائض), whereas it is prohibited in others. This is supported by the narration of al-Rawandi in *al-Khara’ij* from Dawud al-Raqqi from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام in a hadith where he said: "**When dawn broke, he stood and gave the call to prayer and the second call—and he stood me on his right and recited in the first unit *al-Hamd* and *al-Duha*, and in the second *al-Hamd* and *Qul huwa Allahu ahad*. Then he performed the *qunut*, then the salutation, then he sat.**"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لِإِيلافِ قُرَيْشٍ إِيلافِهِمْ رِحْلَةَ الشِّتاءِ وَالصَّيْفِ** **"For the accustomedness of the Quraysh, their accustomedness to the journey of winter and summer" (106:1-2).** The word *ilaf* (إيلاف), with a *kasrah* on the *hamzah*, means gathering with harmony (التئام), as stated by al-Raghib; from it comes the word *ulfah* (ألفة) — *intimacy or familiarity*. It is said in *al-Sihah*: "So-and-so has become accustomed (ألف) to this place," with a *kasrah*, meaning he is familiar with it (يألفه ألفا), and someone else made him accustomed to it (آلفه). It is also said: "I made [myself] accustomed (آلفت) to the place... *ilafan* (إيلافا)." End quote.

The Quraysh are the tribe of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and they are the descendants of al-Nadr ibn Kinanah, who was named Quraysh. The *rihlah* (الرحلة) is the state of traveling on a mount (الراحلة), which is a camel strong enough for travel, as mentioned in *al-Majma‘*. What is meant by the journey is the departure of the Quraysh from Mecca for trade. This was because the Sanctuary (الحرم) is a barren valley with neither crops nor livestock; thus, the Quraysh lived there through trade. They had two trade journeys every year: a journey in the winter to Yemen and a journey in the summer to Syria. They lived by this, and people respected them because of the station of the Sacred House (البيت الحرام), so they did not accost them by cutting off their path or raiding their secure land.

In His saying: **لِإِيلافِ قُرَيْشٍ** "For the accustomedness of the Quraysh," the letter *lam* is for causation (التعليل). The agent (فاعل) of the "accustoming" is Allah, the Glorified, and "Quraysh" is its first object (مفعوله الأول). Its second object is omitted, indicated by what follows. His saying: **إِيلافِهِمْ رِحْلَةَ الشِّتاءِ وَالصَّيْفِ** "their accustomedness to the journey of winter and summer" is an appositive (بدل) for "the accustomedness of the Quraysh." The agent of "their accustomedness" is Allah, its first object is the plural pronoun, and its second object is "the journey..." etc. The underlying meaning is: "For Allah's accustoming the Quraysh to the journey of winter and summer."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلْيَعْبُدُوا رَبَّ هذَا الْبَيْتِ** **"let them worship the Lord of this House" (106:3).** The *fa’* in **فَلْيَعْبُدُوا** "let them worship" is due to an implied conditional meaning (الشرط); i.e., "whatever the case may be, let them worship the Lord of this House for His accustoming [them] during the days of the two journeys." Or it is for an implied elaboration (التفصيل); i.e., "whatever thing there may be, let them worship the Lord of this House..." etc. It is thus like His saying, the Exalted: **وَلِرَبِّكَ فَاصْبِرْ** "and be patient for the sake of your Lord" (74:7).

The sum total of the meaning of the three verses is: Let the Quraysh worship the Lord of this House because of His accustoming them to the journey of winter and summer, while they live thereby in security.

This is considering the Surah as separate from what preceded it, possessing an independent context (سياق) in itself. However, on the premise that it is a part of Surah al-Fil, completing it, they have mentioned that the *lam* in **لِإِيلافِ** is causal, connected to an omitted [verb] indicated by the context. The meaning would be: "We did that to the People of the Elephant as a favor from Us upon the Quraysh, added to Our favor upon them in the journey of winter and summer." It is as if He said: "A favor [added] to a favor." For this reason, it was said that the *lam* conveys the meaning of "to" (إلى), which is the view of al-Farra’.

It was also said: The meaning is, "We did that to the People of the Elephant so that the Quraysh might become familiar (تألف) with Mecca and be able to reside therein," or "so that We might make the Quraysh familiar," for they were terrified of Abrahah when he intended [to attack] it and they fled from him. So We destroyed them so that the Quraysh would return to Mecca and become familiar with it, and Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله would be born and be sent to the people as a bearer of good news and a warner. This is the discussion regarding deriving these meanings from the context.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الَّذِي أَطْعَمَهُمْ مِنْ جُوعٍ وَآمَنَهُمْ مِنْ خَوْفٍ** **"who has fed them [and saved them] from hunger, and secured them from fear" (106:4).** This is an allusion to the clear grace and manifest favor upon them in His accustoming them to the two journeys, which is the provision of food and security. Thus, they live in a land where there is no fertility, nor security for others; so let them worship a Lord who manages their affairs with the best management, and He is the Lord of the House.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لِإِيلافِ قُرَيْشٍ إِيلافِهِمْ**, he said: "It was revealed concerning the Quraysh because their livelihood was from the two journeys—a journey in the winter to Yemen and a journey in the summer to Syria. They would carry from Mecca hides and silver—and what comes from the direction of the sea such as pepper and other things—and they would buy in Syria clothes, fine flour (الدرمك), and grains. They would harmonize (يتألفون) on their path and remain firm in going out—appointing in every excursion a leader from the leaders of the Quraysh—and their livelihood was from that.

When Allah sent His Prophet, they became independent of that—because people came as delegations to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and performed pilgrimage to the House—so Allah said: **فَلْيَعْبُدُوا رَبَّ هذَا الْبَيْتِ ـ الَّذِي أَطْعَمَهُمْ مِنْ جُوعٍ** 'let them worship the Lord of this House—who has fed them from hunger,' so they do not need to go to Syria, **وَآمَنَهُمْ مِنْ خَوْفٍ** 'and secured them from fear,' meaning the fear of the road."

**I say:** His saying, "When Allah sent..." etc., is difficult to reconcile with the context of the Surah's verses. Perhaps it is from the words of al-Qummi, which he took from some of what was narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas.
